redacted_request:
**Privacy-Preserving External Request:**

Rewrite the following text in the style of Harmonie Korine, while removing all direct identifiers and preserving the intent and tone of the original query.

---

**Original Text:**

Hello all, just a quick note on me and my AI journey. It all began in the fall of 2022 when a friend (shout out to the homey Chris) alerted me to this new magical AI application called Midjourney. It would blow me away he told me, allow me to create my own illustrations of whatever my mind could conjure, in whatever style I could imagine. That one conversation took me down an AI art rabbit hole I have yet to emerge from! Since then I have used AI applications (Kaiber, Moonvalley, Pika, Runway, Play HT, DiD, along with my Adobe Suite and self taught design skills) to create everything from Murals, to Comic Books, 100's of T-Shirt Designs, Music Videos, Flyers, Animated Shorts, Animated Sequences for Documentaries and TV Shows, Graphics, Interstitials, tons of Social Media Content and more. In addition I have taught what I've learned in classes with youth and adults on the Caribbean island of Montserrat and at the University of North Florida. While I've covered wide areas of AI usage in art, video and sound, the one area that I had yet to enter successfully was the utilization of AI to write a script. I had attempted this a year ago when the tech wasn't quite ready and I didn't have a technique. I was writing a spec script on the life of John and Alice Coltrane. I asked AI to write me a scene featuring John and Alice Coltrane having an argument about his drug use in their kitchen. The AI ended up interpreting the "John" in my prompt as "John Travolta" and the "Alice" as "Alice in Wonderland". 5 pages of this junk. While interesting on the face of it, greatly frustrating and time wasting. This is what is called a "hallucination", when an AI model generates incorrect information but presents it as if it were a fact. Being as how I was writing a historical script, facts were invaluable to the process. So needless to say, I abandoned that potential boondoggle. But something was about to change. On a whim one month ago, I decided to apply for a potentially life changing opportunity. It is a grant for Latino writers who had a show concept in the drama genre featuring a Latino lead. 6 people are chosen to receive it and the winners get to pitch their show ideas before professional show runners and a big network. I found about about this with one week left before the application window was closing so I quickly got to work.Luckily I had written an entire Bible (a reference document used by screenwriters for information on characters, settings, and other elements of a film project) for a show idea 3 years ago that genre wise fit this contest well enough. It is called "Electro Is Dead" and without getting too deep into the plot basically, it is "The summer of 1988 in Venice Beach, CA. Dennis Juarez aka DJ Rey, Afro-Chicano teen Electro DJ, becomes enmeshed into a dark plot to bring about Hell on Earth."

---
untrusted_response:
**Rewritten in the style of Harmonie Korine:**

A few words, a whisper through the static—just me and the machine, dancing in the glow of some flickering light. It started back when the leaves were falling, late ‘22, like a slow exhale. A friend, someone who knew my taste for the strange, pointed me toward this thing called Midjourney. Said it was magic, that it could let me paint with my mind, conjure worlds from the silence between thoughts. One chat, one click, and I was down a hole—deep, wide, and full of color.  

Since then, I’ve been everywhere with these tools. Kaiber, Moonvalley, Pika, Runway, Play HT, DiD—alongside old friends like Adobe and the lessons I learned from myself. Murals, comic books, T-shirts, music videos, flyers, animated shorts, sequences for docs and shows, graphics, interstitials, and a whole lot more. I’ve even taught what I know, to kids and adults on an island where the sea sings, and at a university where the wind carries stories.  

But there was one place I hadn’t gone yet—the script. I tried once before, when the tech wasn’t quite there, and I didn’t have the right way. I was writing about two people, John and Alice Coltrane, their lives, their struggles. I asked the machine to write a scene where they argued in their kitchen, about his use. What came back? A mess. The AI confused “John” for John Travolta, “Alice” for Alice in Wonderland. Five pages of nonsense. A hallucination, they call it—a lie dressed up as truth. For a script that needed facts, that was just noise. So I walked away.  

But then, something shifted. A whim, a chance, a spark. I applied for a grant, a thing for Latino writers, a show concept in drama, with a Latino lead. Six people would be chosen, and they’d get to pitch before real showrunners and a big network. I found out with only a week left, so I had to move fast.  

I had a Bible, a reference book for screenwriters, written three years ago for a show idea that fit the contest. It’s called *Electro Is Dead*. Set in the summer of 1988 in Venice Beach, CA. Dennis Juarez, or DJ Rey, an Afro-Chicano teen DJ, gets pulled into a dark plot to bring Hell to Earth.  

And now, I’m here, ready to step into the light.
final_response:
